questions.scenario:
A log search shows:

```text
START RequestId: b8e74218-9d2e-4a97-91df
INFO orderId=ord_1042 messageId=5a6f4b79 requestId=b8e74218-9d2e-4a97-91df action=send_receipt
END RequestId: b8e74218-9d2e-4a97-91df
```

Why is the request ID useful?

options.text: It is a stable idempotency key for the business event, so retries will receive the same Lambda request ID.
It links one Lambda invocation to its logs, which helps the team follow one event through one run.
It correlates separate retry invocations automatically, even when each retry receives a new request ID.
It identifies the warm execution environment, so later invocations in the same environment share the value.

questions.explanation:
The Lambda request ID identifies one invocation and appears in the platform start and end records plus application logs. A retried message receives another invocation ID, so the business message or idempotency key is still required across retries. The request ID does not identify a warm environment or control routing.

Learn more in [Lambda context object](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/nodejs-context.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris receipt messages are being delivered more than once during worker retries. The team needs a design that preserves at-least-once processing without sending duplicate receipts.

MessageId is 8b6d4a25. OrderId is ord_1042. ApproximateReceiveCount is 3. Last worker log is provider accepted email, then function timed out before recording success.

What should the worker design protect against?

options.text: Use an idempotency key tied to the business event and make the side effect safe when the same message is delivered again.
Raise memory and timeout to reduce overlap, while leaving duplicate side effects to downstream reconciliation.
Duplicate real-world effects, by checking and recording a stable idempotency key such as receipt:ord_1042.
Set reserved concurrency to one so duplicate deliveries cannot occur for the function.

questions.explanation:
At-least-once delivery means the same job can be attempted again. If the first attempt sent the email but timed out before recording success, the retry must not send a second receipt. Idempotency turns repeated attempts into one business effect.

Learn more in [Lambda asynchronous error handling](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/invocation-async-error-handling.html).

questions.scenario:
The export Lambda logs:

```text
START RequestId: 8f0f0a20
INFO exportId=exp_7732 action=generate_export start
ERROR Task timed out after 3.00 seconds
REPORT Duration: 3000.00 ms Billed Duration: 3000 ms Memory Size: 256 MB Max Memory Used: 182 MB Status: timeout
```

What is the best first response?

options.text: Reduce the SQS batch size without checking whether one record can still exceed the function and downstream timeouts.
Increase the timeout because the batch can complete if each record keeps its current processing time.
Investigate the work duration, batch size, downstream client timeouts, and whether the job fits the function timeout.
Move the unchanged job to a persistent worker before measuring which part of the batch consumes the current timeout.

questions.explanation:
The REPORT line gives a timeout diagnosis and memory clue. The next step is to understand why this unit of work cannot finish in time. Raising timeout may be part of a fix, but it should not be the only thought.

Learn more in [AWS Lambda concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html).

questions.scenario:
A DevPolaris Lambda consumer is increasing downstream throttling during an incident. The on-call engineer must choose controls that reduce pressure without losing queued work.

RequestId is 0ad7729e. MessageId is 5a6f4b79. OrderId is ord_1042. REPORT Duration is 2988 ms Max Memory Used: 251 MB of 256 MB. Error is provider timeout after receipt send accepted.

Which checks belong in a calm diagnostic path?

options.text: Read the first error line and the REPORT line for duration, memory, and timeout clues.
Start by changing timeout, memory, and permissions together so one of them works.
Find the event identifier and request ID in the log group.
Check whether the trigger sent the event shape the handler expects.
Increase reserved concurrency during the incident even though the downstream dependency is already throttling requests.

questions.explanation:
The useful path is evidence first. IDs connect attempts, REPORT lines show runtime pressure, and event shape explains parsing failures. Changing several settings at once makes it harder to know what actually fixed the issue.

Learn more in [CloudWatch Logs concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonCloudWatch/latest/logs/WhatIsCloudWatchLogs.html).

questions.scenario:
A developer tested the DevPolaris receipt Lambda with a hand-written event, but production invokes it through an AWS event source with a different envelope.

{ "orderId": "ord_1042", "email": "maya@example.com", "total": "49.00" }

Production sends this event instead: Records[0].messageId is 5a6f4b79. Records[0].body is {"orderId":"ord_1042","email":"maya@example.com","total":"49.00"}. EventSource is aws:sqs.

The handler fails with `Cannot read properties of undefined (reading body)`. What should be fixed first?

options.text: Inspect an ALB listener first, because the team may be confusing this worker with the HTTP API path.
Update the handler tests and parsing logic to use the real SQS Records shape.
Move the receipt job into the checkout request so the event shape disappears.
Tune the timeout first, because the failure appears during invocation and longer runs may expose more logs.

questions.explanation:
The failure is a contract mismatch between the trigger event and the handler. SQS delivers Records, and the body must be parsed from each record. Timeout and port changes would not fix code that expects the wrong input shape.

Learn more in [Lambda event source mappings](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/invocation-eventsourcemapping.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris publishes receipt-worker Lambda version `18` and moves the `prod` alias from version `17` to version `18`. After five percent of production traffic reaches version `18`, error rate rises while version `17` remains healthy. The team needs an immediate rollback that does not rebuild code or change event-source configuration. What should the release captain do?

options.text: Point the `prod` alias back to version `17`, verify invocation and error metrics, and preserve version `18` evidence for diagnosis.
Delete version `18` first so Lambda automatically routes the alias to the previous published version.
Change the function's unpublished `$LATEST` code because aliases always follow the newest uploaded package.
Recreate the SQS event source mapping against version `17` while leaving the `prod` alias on version `18`.

questions.explanation:
Published versions are immutable release targets, and an alias is the stable pointer that can move between them. Returning `prod` to known-good version `17` restores the previous runtime target without rebuilding or rewiring the event source. Deleting a version does not provide automatic alias rollback, and changing `$LATEST` does not change a versioned alias.

Learn more in [Lambda aliases](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/configuration-aliases.html).

questions.scenario: A DevPolaris receipt Lambda receives an SQS message successfully but fails when it calls Amazon SES. The AWS error names the function's assumed execution role, the denied action `ses:SendEmail`, and the SES identity `orders.devpolaris.com`. Which permission path should the engineer inspect first?

options.text: The Lambda execution role policy and the specific SES identity permission.
The Lambda function resource policy, which controls who may invoke the function rather than what it may call.
The SES sending quota, because quota exhaustion is returned as an IAM authorization denial.
The SQS visibility timeout, because a longer timeout grants the consumer permission to call SES.

questions.explanation:
The function was invoked and SES denied the AWS API action performed by its execution-role session. That makes the execution role's `ses:SendEmail` resource scope the first permission check. A function resource policy controls invocation, while SES quota or SQS timing problems do not appear as this IAM denial tuple.

Learn more in [Lambda execution roles](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/lambda-intro-execution-role.html).
